en.extract:
The bar-backed partridge (Arborophila brunneopectus), also known as the brown-breasted hill-partridge, is a species of partridge in the family Phasianidae. It is found in southwestern China and Southeast Asia.


Taxonomy
English zoologist Edward Blyth first described the bar-backed partridge in 1855.
The bar-backed partridge has three recognized subspecies: 

A. b. albigula (Robinson & Kloss, 1919)
A. b. brunneopectusI (Blyth, 1855)
A. b. henrici (Oustalet,1896)
These subspecies differ in colouration and patterns of their underparts and head, respectively. Some identify the chestnut-bellied partridge and grey-breasted partridge or even the Sichuan partridge or red-breasted partridge as superspecies of the bar-backed partridge.


Description
The bar-backed partridge is typically 280 mm (11 in) long in total, with an average wingspan of 144 mm (5.7 in) for males and 134 mm (5.3 in) for females of the species. They usually have 6–7 cm (2.4–2.8 in) tails, and their bills are about 20–21 mm (0.79–0.83 in) in length. On average, males have a 42 mm (1.7 in) tarsus, while females have 39 mm (1.5 in).
The males are similar in color to females. It is mostly buff in color, with black running from the beak, around the eyes, and to the throat, as well as a black crown and feather tips along its back. It has red eyelids, and its wing feathers are white with black tips, adding brown near the back of the bird.


Distribution and habitat
The bar-backed partridge is found in Cambodia, China, Laos, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam over an estimated 486,000 square kilometres (188,000 sq mi).
Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests and subtropical or tropical moist montane forests. It is typically found at relatively low elevations, under 3,000 feet (910 m), but have been seen at 5,000 feet (1,500 m) and above.


Behaviour

They flock in groups of about 4–9, usually consisting of two parents and their latest offspring or sometimes two families. They feed on seeds, small shells, and insects found in leaves on the forest floor. When disturbed by humans, they quickly scatter into the underbrush. Captive male bar-backed partridges attract a mate by exposing the red feathers around their throat and calling. They breed around May to June, and their 37 by 28 mm (1.5 by 1.1 in) eggs are usually laid in a hole covered with bamboo and grass.


Status
The bar-backed partridge has been evaluated as Least Concern by the IUCN due to its large range; however, its population is slowly declining because of habitat loss and hunting, although this decline is not steep enough to make it Vulnerable.


References


External links

"Arborophila brunneopectus". Avibase.

The '''bar-backed partridge''' ('''''Arborophila brunneopectus'''''), also known as the '''brown-breasted hill-partridge''', is a species of [[partridge]] in the family [[Phasianidae]]. It is found in southwestern [[China]] and [[Southeast Asia]].

==Taxonomy==
English zoologist [[Edward Blyth]] first described the bar-backed partridge in 1855.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021"/>

The bar-backed partridge has three recognized [[subspecies]]:<ref>{{Cite web |title=DOI – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ioc-lists/crossref/ |access-date=2023-03-15 |website=www.worldbirdnames.org |doi=10.14344/ioc.ml.13.1}}</ref> 

* ''A. b. albigula'' <small>(Robinson & Kloss, 1919)</small>
* ''A. b. brunneopectusI'' <small>(Blyth, 1855)</small>
* ''A. b. henrici'' <small>([[Émile Oustalet|Oustalet]],1896)</small>

These subspecies differ in colouration and patterns of their underparts and head, respectively.<ref name="McGowan2010" /> Some identify the [[chestnut-bellied partridge]] and [[grey-breasted partridge]] or even the [[Sichuan partridge]] or [[red-breasted partridge]] as [[superspecies]] of the bar-backed partridge.<ref name="IBC">{{cite web|title=Brown-breasted Hill-partridge (''Arborophila brunneopectus'')|url=http://ibc.lynxeds.com/node/1783|publisher=The Internet Bird Collection|access-date=1 December 2013}}</ref>

==Description==
The bar-backed partridge is typically {{convert|280|mm|abbr=on}} long in total, with an average wingspan of {{convert|144|mm|abbr=on}} for males and {{convert|134|mm|abbr=on}} for females of the species. They usually have {{convert|6|-|7|cm|abbr=on}} tails, and their bills are about {{convert|20|-|21|mm|abbr=on}} in length. On average, males have a {{convert|42|mm|abbr=on}} [[tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]], while females have {{convert|39|mm|abbr=on}}.<ref name="JBNHS">{{cite journal|title=Arboricola brunneopecta|journal=[[Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society]]|publisher=The Times Press|year=1923|volume=28|pages=13–15|url=https://archive.org/stream/journalofbombayn2812192122bomb#page/12/mode/2up/|access-date=1 December 2013|location=[[Bombay]]}}</ref>

The males are similar in color to females. It is mostly [[buff (color)|buff]] in color, with black running from the beak, around the eyes, and to the throat, as well as a black crown and feather tips along its back. It has red eyelids, and its wing feathers are white with black tips, adding brown near the back of the bird.<ref name="Blanford1889">{{cite book|last1=Blanford|first1=W. T.|author-link1=William Thomas Blanford|last2=Oates|first2=E. W.|author-link2=Eugene William Oates|title=The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma|year=1889|publisher=[[Taylor & Francis]]|location=London|volume=4|pages=128–129|url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsindia04oaterich#page/128|access-date=1 December 2013}}</ref><ref name="Ogilvie1893">{{cite book|last=Ogilvie-Grant|first=W. R.|title=Catalogue of the Game-Birds in the Collection of the British Museum|year=1893|location=London|pages=216–217|url=https://archive.org/stream/catalogueofbirds22brit#page/216|author-link=William Robert Ogilvie-Grant|access-date=1 December 2013}}</ref><ref name="Deignan1945">{{cite book|last=Deignan|first=H. G.|author-link=Herbert Girton Deignan|title=The Birds of Northern Thailand|year=1945|publisher=[[United States Government Printing Office]]|location=Washington|pages=89–91|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7642783|access-date=1 December 2013}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The bar-backed partridge is found in [[Cambodia]], [[China]], [[Laos]], [[Myanmar]], [[Thailand]], and [[Vietnam]] over an estimated {{convert|486000|km2}}.<ref name="BirdLife">{{cite web|title=Bar-backed Partridge ''Arborophila brunneopectus''|url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=213|publisher=[[BirdLife International]]|access-date=1 December 2013}}</ref>

Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s. It is typically found at relatively low elevations, under {{convert|3000|ft}}, but have been seen at {{convert|5000|ft}} and above.<ref name="JBNHS" />

==Behaviour==
[[File:Arborophila brunneopectus pair feeding - Kaeng Krachan.ogv|thumb|left|Two Bar-backed Partridges feeding]]
They flock in groups of about 4–9, usually consisting of two parents and their latest offspring or sometimes two families. They feed on seeds, small shells, and insects found in leaves on the [[forest floor]]. When disturbed by humans, they quickly scatter into the underbrush. Captive male bar-backed partridges attract a mate by exposing the red feathers around their throat and calling. They breed around May to June, and their {{convert|37|by|28|mm|abbr=on}} eggs are usually laid in a hole covered with bamboo and grass.<ref name="McGowan2010">{{cite book|last1=McGowan|first1=Phil|last2=Madge|first2=Steve|author-link2=Steve Madge|title=Pheasants, Partridges & Grouse: Including buttonquails, sandgrouse and allies|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=07PtbfrpsdAC&pg=PT171|year=2010|publisher=[[Bloomsbury Publishing]]|isbn=978-1-4081-3566-2|pages=171–172}}</ref><ref name="JBNHS" /><ref name="Hume1875">{{cite journal|last=Hume|first=Allan|author-link=Allan Octavian Hume|title=Arborophila brunneopectus|url=https://archive.org/stream/strayfeathersjou31875hume#page/174|journal=Stray Feathers|year=1875|volume=3|pages=174–176|access-date=1 December 2013}}</ref>

==Status==
The bar-backed partridge has been evaluated as [[Least Concern]] by the [[IUCN]] due to its large range; however, its population is slowly declining because of [[Habitat destruction|habitat loss]] and hunting, although this decline is not steep enough to make it [[Vulnerable species|Vulnerable]].<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /><ref name="McGowan1995">{{cite book|last1=McGowan|first1=Philip J. K.|last2=Dowell|first2=Simon D.|last3=Caroll|first3=John P.|last4=Aebischer|first4=Nicholas J.|title=Partridges, Quails, Francolins, Snowcocks, and Guineafowl|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kj3rh2uD00oC&pg=PA42|year=1995|publisher=IUCN|isbn=978-2-8317-0269-8|page=42|access-date=1 December 2013}}</ref>

zh.extract:
褐胸山鹧鸪（学名：Arborophila brunneopectus）又名斑背山鹧鸪，为雉科山鹧鸪属的鸟类。多见于平原和低山的常绿森林和竹林。该物种的模式产地在缅甸丹那沙林。


亚种
褐胸山鹧鸪指名亚种（学名：Arborophila brunneopectus brunneopectus）。在中国大陆，分布于云南、广西等地。该物种的模式产地在缅甸丹那沙林。
